blocks.content: He walked back. As he passed the house he glanced up — habit, the same involuntary attention to environment he'd had since childhood. Second floor. Window facing south.
The crime scene tape was visible through the glass. A yellow edge catching the afternoon light.

He looked at the window. Then at the exterior wall below it. The porch roof that ran along the back of the house — maybe eight feet below the window ledge. The guttering. The drainpipe on the near corner.
He moved to get a better angle. Stepped off the path and into the narrow strip of beach between the two houses. Looked up.

From the window ledge to the porch roof — eight feet drop, if that. And the drainpipe bracket on the near side, the kind with enough purchase to take a foot if you knew it was there. If you'd measured it. If you'd been looking for exactly this.
He didn't move closer. He didn't touch anything. He just looked.
Then he took his phone out and photographed the exterior wall from where he stood. The window. The porch roof. The drainpipe. The bracket.
He had Davenport's number in his phone now.
He composed a text: *Look at the exterior wall below the south-facing window. Specifically the porch roof and the gutter bracket on the near corner. Then call me.*
He sent it. He went inside. He added a new page to the legal pad.
At the top he wrote: *The door was never the way in. The window was.*
